text: The endoscopic view shows a white ulcer at the gastroesophageal junction (G-junction) with grade II gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Narrow band imaging highlights the ulcer at the G-junction.
Narrowband imaging shows no mucosal abnormality or change in mucosal pattern at the GI junction. However, there is visible continuous reflux of subarachnoid tissue and a paraesophageal hernia.

The laparoscopic view reveals a large paraesophageal hernia with the stomach herniated into the chest, surrounded by numerous adhesions. The surgical team begins addressing the tense adhesions on the right side.
The surgeon begins by removing adhesions near the right crus of the diaphragm, preparing to dissect the hernia sac on the left side.

The surgeon encounters very tense adhesions and begins a careful dissection. The stomach is densely adherent to the surrounding tissue due to a previous operation. The dissection is focused on the right side, just below the right crus of the diaphragm, as the surgeon attempts to push the stomach towards the patient's left side and access the chest through the hiatus. A harmonic scalpel is used for dissection.

The surgeon dissects dense adhesions surrounding the hernia sac, carefully separating the tissue planes anteriorly near the pericardium. The procedure then shifts to the left side, where additional adhesions to the hernia sac are visible.

The surgeon visualizes the edge of the hernia sac and performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel to reduce the sac completely. The stomach fundus is visible herniated into the chest, and care is taken to identify structures to avoid cutting vital tissues.

The surgeon carefully preserves the anterior vagus nerve while addressing remaining adhesions. The vagus nerve is visible extending into the chest, and omental adhesions are seen attaching to the diaphragm from the lateral aspect.
The surgeon completes the removal of adhesions on the left side and begins dissecting the hernia sac at the two o'clock position to facilitate its reduction.
The surgeon shifts and separates the truss from the hernia sac, initiating dissection between the sac and the pleura.

The surgeon identifies a herniation of stomach contents and omentum into the chest through a small rent in the hernia sac. The hernia sac is being dissected, with the edge visible above the pericardium.
The surgeon cuts the hernia sac on the right side, with nearly half of the sac now reduced. The attachment of the sac is first dissected and then cut, with the anterior aspect visible.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect and remove attachments of the hernia sac at the ten o'clock position, while avoiding injury to the esophagus and surrounding veins. The esophagus is pulled to the right side, and some esophageal muscle is cut during the resection. The remaining part of the sac attached to the pericardium is meticulously excised. A precision scope is used to clearly visualize anatomical structures, including lymph nodes.

The surgeon dissects the omentum that has permeated into the chest, focusing on the lower left cusp. The short gastric vessel is sealed and cut using a harmonic device. Dense adhesions are carefully lysed to move the omental contents down into the abdomen, while minimizing cutting of the peritoneum over the crus.
The surgeon carefully preserves the peritoneum over the left crus to avoid exposing the underlying muscles. The omentum, along with the hernia sac, is reduced and removed from the chest cavity.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac on the left side, cutting peripheral attachments to facilitate its descent. The anterior attachment is also addressed, with approximately three-fourths of the sac now lowered, though some attachments remain.

The surgeon carefully dissects and separates the adhesions layer by layer near the aorta, avoiding injury to surrounding structures. The sac is nearly fully detached, and the attachments near the dura are being cut.

After reducing the contents, the surgeon successfully mobilizes a significant portion of the esophagus into the abdomen. The remaining attachments at the gastroesophageal junction are carefully dissected using a harmonic scalpel. The sac is nearly fully dissected, and the short gastric vessels around the fundus and body of the stomach are ligated.
The surgeon frees the fundus of the stomach from the left side, using ligature to manage attachments around the left crus of the diaphragm. The aorta and esophagus are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection on the right side, addressing remaining attachments. The right hepatic and left gastric vessels are visible during this process.

The surgeon releases attachments to free the lower part of the esophagus, ensuring it is fully mobilized. The posterior attachments of the esophagus are addressed, with careful dissection away from the posterior vagus nerve, extending the dissection into the chest cavity.

The surgeon encounters dense adhesions at the OG junction and places an umbilical tape around it to facilitate maneuvering the esophagus and stomach. Using ligature, the adhesions below the esophagus are released, allowing the esophagus to be separated from surrounding tissues up to the pericardium, extending seven to eight centimeters into the chest. The aorta is visible below the esophagus, and the anterior vagus nerve is dissected, running towards the patient's left shoulder.

The surgeon carefully avoids injuring the vagus nerve while addressing remaining adhesion bands at the upper end of the pericardium using a ligature. Direct vessels from the aorta are visible, and attention is focused on managing these structures.

The surgeon separates attachments around the vena cava and the right crus of the diaphragm, addressing adhesions near the caudate lobe and spleen using a ligature. The forceps are opened to reveal a large defect, with the right crus appearing thinned out, approximately six centimeters below the crura.

The surgeon places a mesh over the surgical site and uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect attachments around the caudate lobe, vena cava, and right crus of the diaphragm. Blunt dissection is performed between the vena cava and right crus using harmonic and bowel forceps to separate adhesions. Suturing of the right crus is initiated using a non-absorbable V-Loc suture.

The surgeon uses a non-absorbable V-lock suture to perform continuous suturing on the right crus of the diaphragm, achieving complete approximation despite the thinning of the tissue. The suturing is facilitated by prior dissection near the vena cava.

The surgeon completes the second layer of suturing using a V-lock suture, ensuring the approximation of the tissue around the esophagus. An intraoperative endoscopy is performed, revealing a small muscle gap and dense adhesions near the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a 15 by 10 centimeter Paratex composite mesh, which is a soft polyester mesh, and evenly spreads it over the surgical area. The mesh is fixed in place using Ethibond 2-0 sutures, and excess mesh extending toward the spleen is trimmed. The lateral margin of the mesh is sutured securely.

The surgeon cuts the excess mesh near the lower part of the crura that was extending over the stomach. The remaining mesh is sutured to the dura. The esophagus is visible, extending into the stomach with a length of approximately five to six centimeters.

The surgeon places the falciform ligament between the esophagus and the mesh to prevent complications. Despite the patient's low weight making the falciform less bulky, it is spread using a box-type suture with Echoball 2-0, ensuring it covers the crura and mesh effectively.

The falciform ligament is sutured to the crura at two locations to ensure it spreads adequately, covering a small portion of the mesh. Despite normal stomach motility, the surgeon proceeds with a partial fundoplication, performing a two-page fundoplication.
The surgeon positions the wrap just above the gastroesophageal junction, securing it to the crura below the falciform ligament using umbilical tape to prevent rotation of the wrap.

The surgeon performs a partial fundoplication, securing sutures on the left side of the patient at three locations. The wrap, approximately three centimeters in size, is fixed to the crura to prevent contact between the esophagus and the mesh. The upper edge of the wrap is sutured to the crura for stabilization, and the Deaver retractor is removed.

The surgeon spreads the mesh over the anterior aspect of the crura, pulling it through an incision above the left lobe of the liver on the diaphragm. The mesh is then spread over the diaphragm and sutured in place with a stay suture, taking care not to penetrate too deeply due to proximity to the pericardium. The heartbeat is visible, and the patient tolerates the procedure well. The port sites are visible.
